message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12
Recent Green phase: Added mainItemPremium(item) which applies additive surcharges to a main item's base premium: first-insurance (FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE 0.1, always) and curse (CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE 0.5, if item.cursed). The main-items reduce now uses mainItemPremium. Component pricing still uses withFirstInsurance (base * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = base*1.1).

There is now a representation inconsistency: main items use the additive rate model (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE + ...), while components use the multiplicative withFirstInsurance (FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER 1.1). For first-insurance alone these are numerically identical (base*1.1 == base*(1+0.1)). Consider unifying so both paths express first-insurance the same way (single source of truth for the 10% first-insurance rate), reducing duplication/confusion. 

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Do NOT add behavior for future tests (no enchantment surcharge, no loyalty/follow-up, no claim logic) — but DO consolidate the first-insurance representation if it improves clarity and removes the duplicate concept.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now let me work through the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase prompt explicitly identifies the issue: there are now two representations of first-insurance.

- `mainItemPremium` uses additive rates: `base * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE + curseRate)` where `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1`
- `withFirstInsurance` uses a multiplier: `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` where `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`

These are two names/constants for the *same concept* (10% first-insurance surcharge). This is conceptual duplication (Rule 3 violation) and a single-source-of-truth problem. `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` is a redundant constant derivable from `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`.

Naming-wise, `withFirstInsurance` describes a multiplicative wrapping while `mainItemPremium` uses additive rates. Unifying them on the additive rate model gives a single source of truth for the 10% rate.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the two relevant functions/constants that express first-insurance:

**`withFirstInsurance` + `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`:**
```
const FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1;                       // constant 1.1 (1) + binding (1) = 2
const withFirstInsurance = (basePremium) =>
  basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER;
```
- Bindings: `withFirstInsurance`, `basePremium`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (ref) = 3
- Invocations: `*` = 1 → 2
- Constant: `1.1` = 1
- Constant binding `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` = 1
- Subtotal mass ≈ 3 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 7

**`mainItemPremium` (uses `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`):**
```
const FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1;
const mainItemPremium = (item) => {
  const base = BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
  const curseRate = item.cursed ? CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;
  return base * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE + curseRate);
};
```
- Constant `0.1` (1) + binding (1) = 2
- This function stays largely unchanged.

The duplication lies in maintaining BOTH `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER (1.1)` and `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE (0.1)` as independent magic numbers representing the same 10% rate.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 12 green. 
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Two different idioms for the same surcharge obscures the shared concept.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` and `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` are duplicate knowledge of the same 10%. This is the target.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Removing the redundant multiplier constant reduces elements.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll unify both paths on the additive `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (single source of truth) and rewrite `withFirstInsurance` to derive from it, eliminating `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`.

content.text:
All 12 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change removed `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (one binding + one constant ≈ mass 2) and replaced `withFirstInsurance`'s body `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` with `basePremium * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE)`.

- Removed: constant binding `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1` → -2 mass (one binding, one constant `1.1`)
- `withFirstInsurance` body: was 1 ref (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`) + 1 invocation (`*`). Now: 1 constant (`1`) + 1 ref (`FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`) + 2 invocations (`+`, `*`). Net local change ≈ +1 constant +1 invocation = +3 mass, but it now shares the rate concept.

Net mass: roughly neutral to slightly higher locally (~+1), but **conceptual duplication eliminated** — the 10% rate now lives in exactly one constant. Per the guidance, Rule 2/Rule 3 (single source of truth, reveals intent) trumps a tiny APP mass increase.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 3 — No Duplication, single source of truth):
- Removed the redundant `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1` constant.
- Rewrote `withFirstInsurance` to derive the surcharge from `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` as `basePremium * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE)`, matching the additive model already used by `mainItemPremium`.

**Benefits:**
- The 10% first-insurance rate now has exactly one definition (`FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`). Previously the same fact lived in two independent magic numbers (`1.1` and `0.1`) that could drift apart.
- Both pricing paths (main items and components) now express first-insurance identically — the conceptual inconsistency flagged in the Green phase is resolved.
- Rule 4: one fewer constant/element.

**Mass note:** Local mass is roughly neutral (≈+1 from the additive form), accepted because Rule 3 (eliminating duplicate knowledge) and Rule 2 (consistent representation) take priority over a negligible APP increase.

I did **not** add any future behavior (no enchantment surcharge, loyalty/follow-up, or claim logic).

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: Unified first-insurance representation — removed redundant
             FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER (1.1); withFirstInsurance now derives
             from FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE as base*(1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE),
             matching mainItemPremium. Single source of truth for the 10% rate.
Mass Change: ~neutral (≈ +1 locally; accepted — Rule 3/Rule 2 over APP)
Tests: All 12 passing
```

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_18-30-07_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

The conceptual duplication between the additive and multiplicative first-insurance models has been consolidated; both paths now reference the single `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` constant. All 12 tests remain green.
